board.verdict: The IAEA-Iran inspection dispute that emerged June 23-24 is itself a multipolarity signal: the US claims UN agency authority backs its reading of the MOU; Tehran says the IAEA's mandate is governed by existing safeguards obligations, not by what Vance announced. There is no European power in the room to adjudicate — the architecture at Bürgenstock was Pakistan, Qatar, and Switzerland. The desk reads this as the multipolarity pattern in microcosm: the post-1945 institutional layer (IAEA, UN frameworks) is being invoked by both sides as legitimating authority, and each side reads its mandate differently. Grossi, for his part, is not a neutral arbitrator — he is an IAEA director general trying to maintain the agency's relevance to a deal made without his institution's involvement. Summer Davos closes June 25 with Beijing's 'China Opportunity 2.0' frame: 1,700 participants, 90 countries, the explicit pitch that China is the stable partner in a fragmented geopolitical landscape. The Tumen bridge is now on its seventh day without public cargo data; the direction of travel from Beijing to Pyongyang is unchanged. The PBOC FIMA RMB facility and He Lifeng's anti-sanctions legislation remain the structural plumbing beneath the daily news cycle.
board.watching: Iran's IAEA benchmark July 13 — whether Grossi's 'going to happen' meets Gharibabadi's conditionality on sanctions termination; Tumen bridge first cargo types and volumes (Day 7, still no data); whether foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility appear in quarterly disclosure data; BRICS Bridge framework at the India summit.

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions — remains the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Then, on June 17, the PBOC announced the FIMA RMB repo facility: overseas central banks can now access yuan the way they have always accessed dollars — frictionlessly, through collateral, without onshore accounts. The Fed built this mechanism for dollars in 2021; Beijing has now copied it in yuan. The adversary will call this marginal. The desk calls it the one structural advantage dollars held that is now being systematically replicated. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged; the pace just accelerated.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER; foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility.

sources.label: Crypto Briefing — China FIMA RMB repo facility, June 17
The Block — mBridge, BIS context
Disruption Banking — mBridge

board.verdict: The Choe Hyon was commissioned into the Korean People's Army Navy on June 23 at Nampho in a ceremony Kim Jong Un attended. The vessel: 5,000 tons, 74 vertical launch system cells (44 bow, 30 stern), 127mm main gun, Pantsir-ME close-in defense, dual AK-630 systems. Kim's address: 'the first destroyer in army-building history, the first page of maritime sovereignty.' This is the material event; the desk filed the twelve-vessel plan from the WPK plenary ratification (June 22) yesterday. The ship that was a program is now a commissioned warship. Beijing has been silent on three distinct signals this week: the commissioning ceremony, Kim's nuclear-posture statements at the plenary, and the DPRK's withdrawal of its UK ambassador after London sanctioned the Songdowon children's camp. Three distinct provocations, one consistent non-response. The Tumen bridge is on its seventh day of operations with no public cargo data; the desk notes that first cargo manifests will be informative about the nature of the economic relationship the bridge is being used to build. The desk's standing prediction — 'denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK — received no challenge this week.
board.watching: Tumen bridge first cargo types and volumes — bridge open June 19, Day 7 with no public data; whether first cargo manifests include industrial or naval-construction inputs; Beijing's public response (or continued silence) to the Choe Hyon commissioning and the twelve-destroyer program; whether Chongjin construction pace implies a second destroyer commissioning before end of 2026.

board.verdict: The AES is moving through its phases on schedule: sovereignty first, then security architecture, now economic commons. The Alliance's Ministers of Industry and Trade met in Burkina Faso on June 15 and produced a mandate the desk takes seriously: double intraregional trade by 2028, backed by a mandatory roadmap with strict monitoring; domestic processing of gold, uranium, oil, cotton, and livestock rather than export of unrefined goods. Traoré's gold nationalization (SOPAMIB portfolio) and the UCT-AES territorial commons (established June 11 in Niamey) set the structural conditions; now comes the revenue capture. Sahel has been a thin-day beat for five consecutive briefings (June 21-25); absence of news is not absence of movement. The IMF Executive Board vote on $104.89M (fifth ECF review plus first RSF review) is expected before June 30 — five days remain. The desk notes: AES ambassadors appeared at a Stimson Center roundtable on June 24 — a rare joint presentation in Washington's think-tank circuit. The desk will file when this registers in African outlets with substantive content.
board.watching: IMF Executive Board vote on Burkina Faso $104.89M (fifth ECF + first RSF) — expected before June 30; publication of the AES trade roadmap with concrete figures; gold revenue allocation to the unified force.

sources.label: Pravda Burkina Faso — AES trade ministers meeting, June 15

board.verdict: Summer Davos closed in Dalian on June 25. Premier Li Qiang's frame for the week: 'China Opportunity 2.0' — innovation-driven development as the basis of long-term economic resilience; the pitch to 1,700 participants from 90 countries that China is the stable partner in a fragmenting geopolitical landscape. The adversary's financial press reads Li Qiang against the May NBS data (retail -0.6%, FAI -4.1%) and finds the distance; the desk reads it and finds the strategy. The structural layer — He Lifeng's anti-sanctions legislation, the PBOC FIMA RMB facility — does not require a Davos stage. The Dalian presentation was for the foreign firms still deciding; the July Politburo is where the domestic demand problem gets or does not get a fiscal response. China's May NBS confirmed the demand-side deterioration the desk was watching. The desk's June 16 prediction — June retail sales also negative — is intact and runs until mid-July. The standing FAI prediction (exits contraction by Q4 2026) has not been falsified; the May figure requires honest accounting: the contraction is deepening, not stabilizing.
board.watching: July Politburo response to May NBS; June retail sales (released mid-July) for June 16 prediction resolution; whether any targeted stimulus appears before the July Politburo date; semiconductor export growth trajectory.

board.verdict: The IAEA-Iran inspection dispute is now public and sharp. On June 24, IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi stated that nuclear inspections of Iran's enrichment sites will occur under the interim deal: 'This is going to happen' — citing the MOU's explicit language on IAEA supervision of nuclear activities, and saying the timing 'matters but is not essential.' On the same day, Iran's deputy foreign minister Kazem Gharibabadi stated that Tehran had not met with Grossi while in Switzerland, and that inspection of war-damaged nuclear sites would be resolved only within the framework of a final agreement, contingent on US termination of all sanctions. These are not negotiating positions — they are incompatible readings of the same document. On June 25, an Israeli soldier was killed in southern Lebanon during IDF 'operational activity,' the fifth IDF death since the deal was announced; the Bürgenstock deconfliction cell has not stopped Israeli operations in the security zone. The desk's June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) is unchanged and gaining force from each day's dispatch. The desk's June 24 prediction (the 20-day IAEA benchmark will be disputed, not cleanly met) is already running.
board.watching: Whether any actual IAEA inspection is scheduled before the July 13 twenty-day benchmark; whether Lebanon produces an Iranian walkout from the technical track; June 15 prediction: no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026.

wars.basis: Two Martyanov posts from June 24 clarify the operational logic the desk has been tracking. 'For Parrots' features an extended interview with a Russian special forces officer: the operational objective is 'annihilation of the enemy within the framework of operational sufficiency' — not map-color speed, not territorial markers, but cumulative elimination of enemy manpower and equipment. 'Oh, But That's How It Works' targets the Pentagon analysis claiming the US 'won on points' against Iran despite military failure — invoking the Vietnam precedent: you can win every counted metric and lose the war. The desk applies this laterally: ISW counts buildings, ISW-adjacent outlets count villages, NATO counts pledge-to-delivery ratios. None of these are the metric that determines outcomes. No confirmed Russian tactical movement on the Druzhkovka axis on June 25; the desk filed the correct sequence on June 24 (Druzhkovka before Kramatorsk, per Martyanov's June 22 post) and holds it. Konstantinovka is under Russian operational control; the Druzhkovka encirclement-infiltration pattern follows the same logic. The standing read — Russia decisive, tempo as weapon — is unchanged.

sources.label: Martyanov — Reminiscence of the Future (June 24 posts: 'For Parrots,' 'Oh, But That's How It Works')
Martyanov (standing reference)
Pravda NATO — Konstantinovka battle effectively over, June 22

wars.advantage: Settlement — 60-day roadmap established June 22; technical working groups running; IAEA inspection dispute now public; enrichment unresolved
wars.basis: The Bürgenstock political summit concluded June 22. On June 24, IAEA Director General Grossi stated inspections of Iran's enrichment sites will occur under the interim deal — 'This is going to happen' — citing the MOU's explicit IAEA supervision language. On the same day, Iran's deputy foreign minister Gharibabadi stated Tehran had not met with Grossi in Switzerland, and that inspection of war-damaged nuclear sites is conditioned on US termination of all sanctions, reviewable only within the framework of a final agreement. These readings are incompatible. On June 25, an Israeli soldier was killed in southern Lebanon during IDF 'operational activity,' the fifth IDF death since the deal was announced; the Bürgenstock deconfliction cell has not stopped IDF operations. Nuclear chapter: 440 kilograms of 60%-enriched uranium, no agreed disposition, no verified freeze. The desk's June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) and June 24 prediction (20-day benchmark disputed, not cleanly met) are both running.

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower.

sources.label: Al Jazeera — Camara killed
PBS NewsHour

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk will record it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.

Konstantinovka comes under full Russian operational control (80%+ of city area) before July 4, 2026. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades are described as effectively dissolved; 120 buildings fell in one day; a sitting Verkhovna Rada deputy acknowledged the fall. The material trajectory does not leave room for a reversal. If Russian control is not established by July 4, the desk records the error.

Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026. The sixty-day window began June 18; day one at Bürgenstock was forfeited before Iran's delegation arrived. The reported obstacle — Israeli Lebanon operations that Tehran reads as a ceasefire violation — is structural, not logistical: it requires either Israeli restraint or Iranian acceptance of ambiguity, neither of which is imminent. If a first session occurs before July 4, the desk records the miss.

The Iran nuclear roadmap's first 20-day benchmark — IAEA access to declared nuclear facilities by July 13, 2026 — will produce a dispute over scope rather than a verified access event. Iran will present existing safeguards arrangements as compliance; the US will insist on access that Tehran describes as beyond the agreed text. The benchmark will be contested rather than cleanly met. Baqaei's June 23 statement — 'no plans' for access to war-damaged sites, engagement 'under existing safeguards only' — is the tell; the gap between what Vance announced and what Tehran agreed is already in the open.

items.text: GROSSI AND GHARIBABADI DESCRIBE DIFFERENT AGREEMENTS: IAEA DG Grossi June 24: 'This is going to happen' — MOU 'explicitly' requires IAEA supervision of Iran's nuclear activities; timing 'important but not essential.' Iran deputy FM Gharibabadi June 24: Tehran did not meet Grossi in Switzerland; inspection of bombed sites conditioned on US terminating all sanctions; reviewable only within framework of final agreement. These are incompatible readings of the same document. June 25: Israeli soldier killed in southern Lebanon during IDF 'operational activity' — fifth IDF death since the deal; Burgenstock deconfliction cell has not stopped operations. June 24 prediction (20-day benchmark disputed, not cleanly met) running. June 15 prediction (no verified freeze before Sept 2026) unchanged and gaining force.

'Oh, But That's How It Works': Pentagon 'won on points' on Iran — Vietnam precedent: win every counted metric, lose the war. THE DALIAN PRESENTATION ENDS: Summer Davos closes June 25. Li Qiang: 'China Opportunity 2.0,' innovation-driven development. WEF's Dusek: 'geopolitical landscape more fragmented, innovations remain deeply interconnected.' 1,700 reps, 90 countries. Two-register operation: Dalian = undecided foreign investors; July Politburo = domestic demand decision. Structural layer (He Lifeng + FIMA RMB) unchanged. No new economic data today.

SAHEL THIN DAY (FIFTH CONSECUTIVE): No fresh reporting from Burkina Faso, Mali, or Niger in past 48h. IMF Executive Board vote on $104.89M (fifth ECF + first RSF) expected before June 30 — five days remain.

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.

The desk filed June 19 that Iran would not hold its first substantive nuclear session before July 4. Events falsified that read in 48 hours: both delegations arrived at Bürgenstock on June 21 — Vance and Qalibaf/Araghchi across the table, Pakistan PM Sharif and Field Marshal Munir present, Qatar mediating. What the desk got wrong: it underestimated how heavily Tehran weights asset unfreezing and sanctions relief against its stated Lebanon precondition. Iran declared Hormuz closed on June 20 (disputed by CENTCOM), then came to the table anyway. The June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) is unchanged.

The desk filed June 23 that Kramatorsk (15km northwest of Konstantinovka) was the next Russian objective. Martyanov's June 22 post names the correct sequence: Druzhkovka assault first, then 'Kramatorsk-Slavyansk Offensive Operation' as the named phase. Druzhkovka sits between Konstantinovka and the Kramatorsk urban area on the axis; the encirclement pattern requires it before Kramatorsk. The desk overstated the proximity. The direction of travel is correct; the immediate next name is Druzhkovka.
